From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American philosophical movement

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/American_modernism "Special:EditPage/American modernism") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["American modernism"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22American+modernism%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22American+modernism%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22American+modernism%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22American+modernism%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22American+modernism%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22American+modernism%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**American modernism**, much like the [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") movement in general, is a trend of philosophical thought arising from the widespread changes in culture and society in the age of [modernity](/wiki/Modernity "Modernity"). American modernism is an artistic and cultural movement in the United States beginning at the turn of the 20th century, with a core period between World War I and World War II. Like its European counterpart, American modernism stemmed from a rejection of [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") thinking, seeking to better represent reality in a new, more industrialized world.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **is written like a [personal reflection, personal essay, or argumentative essay](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Wikipedia_is_not_a_publisher_of_original_thought "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not")** that states a Wikipedia editor's personal feelings or presents an original argument about a topic. Please [help improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit) by rewriting it in an [encyclopedic style](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Information_style_and_tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles"). *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Characteristically, [modernist](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") art has a tendency to abstraction, is innovative, aesthetic, futuristic and self-referential. It includes [visual art](/wiki/Visual_art "Visual art"), [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature"), music, [film](/wiki/Modernist_film "Modernist film"), [design](/wiki/Design "Design"), [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") as well as [life style](/wiki/Life_style_(sociology) "Life style (sociology)"). It reacts against [historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)"), artistic conventions and [institutionalization](/wiki/Institutionalization "Institutionalization") of art. Art was not only to be dealt with in academies, theaters or concert halls, but to be included in everyday life and accessible for everybody. Furthermore, cultural institutions concentrated on [fine art](/wiki/Fine_art "Fine art") and scholars paid little attention to the revolutionary styles of modernism. Economic and technological progress in the U.S. during the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties") gave rise to widespread [utopianism](/wiki/Utopianism "Utopianism"), which influenced some modernist artists, while others were skeptical of the embrace of technology. The victory in World War I confirmed the status of the U.S. as an international player and gave the people self-confidence and a feeling of security. In this context, American modernism marked the beginning of American art as distinct and autonomous from European taste, by breaking artistic conventions that had been shaped after European traditions until then.

American modernism benefited from the diversity of immigrant cultures. Artists were inspired by African, Caribbean, Asian and European folk cultures and embedded these exotic styles in their works.

The Modernist American movement was a reflection of American life in the 20th century. In the quickly industrializing world and hastened pace of life, it was easy for the individual to be swallowed up by the vastness of things, left wandering, devoid of purpose. Social boundaries in race, class, sex, wealth and religion were being challenged. As the social structure was challenged by new incoming views, the bounds of traditional standards and social structure dissolved, and a loss of identity was what remained, translating eventually into isolation, alienation and an overall feeling of separateness from any kind of "whole". The unity of a war-rallied country was dying, along with it the illusion of the pleasantries it sold to its soldiers and people. The world was left violent, vulgar and spiritually empty.

The middle class worker fell into a distinctly unnoticeable position, a cog much too small to hope to find recognition in a much greater machine. Citizens were overcome with their own futility. Youths' dreams shattered with failure and a disillusioning disappointment in recognition of limit and loss. The lives of the disillusioned and outcasts became more focal. Ability to define self through hard work and resourcefulness, to create your own vision of yourself without the help of traditional means, became prized. Some authors endorsed this, while others, such as F. Scott Fitzgerald, challenged how alluring but destructively false the values of privilege can be.

Modernist America had to find common ground in a world no longer unified in belief. The unity found lay in the common ground of the shared consciousness within all human experience. The importance of the individual was emphasized; the truly limited nature of the human experience formed a bond across all bridges of race, class, sex, wealth or religion. Society, in this way, found shared meaning, even in disarray.

Some see modernism in the tradition of 19th century [aestheticism](/wiki/Aestheticism "Aestheticism") and the "[art for art's sake](/wiki/Art_for_art%27s_sake "Art for art's sake")" movement. [Clement Greenberg](/wiki/Clement_Greenberg "Clement Greenberg") argues that modernist art excludes "anything outside itself". Others see modernist art, for example in [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") music, as a medium for emotions and moods, and many works dealt with contemporary issues, like feminism and city life. Some artists and theoreticians even added a political dimension to American modernism.

American modernist design and architecture enabled people to lead a modern life. Work and family life changed radically and rapidly due to the economic upswing during the 1920s. In the U.S., the car became popular and affordable for many, leisure time and entertainment gained importance and the job market opened up for women. In order to make life more efficient, designers and architects aimed at the simplification of housework.

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") at the end of the '20s and during the '30s disillusioned people about the economic stability of the country and eroded utopianist thinking. The outbreak and the terrors of World War II caused further changes in mentality. The [Post-war](/wiki/Post-war "Post-war") period that followed was termed [Late Modernism](/wiki/Late_Modernism "Late Modernism").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The *[Postmodernist](/wiki/Postmodernist "Postmodernist")* era was generally considered characteristic of the art of the late 20th century beginning in the 1980s.

## Visual arts

There is no single date for the beginning of the modern era in America, as dozens of painters were active at the beginning of the 20th century. It was the time when the first [cubist](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism") landscapes ([Precisionism](/wiki/Precisionism "Precisionism")), still-life and portraits appeared; bright colors entered the palettes of painters, and the first [non-objective](/wiki/Geometric_abstraction "Geometric abstraction") paintings were displayed in the galleries.

The modernist movement during the formative years was also becoming popular in New York City by 1913 at the popular Manhattan studio gallery of [Wilhelmina Weber Furlong](/wiki/Wilhelmina_Weber_Furlong "Wilhelmina Weber Furlong") (1878–1962) and through the work of the Whitney Studio Club in 1918.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) According to Davidson, the beginning of American modernist painting can be dated to the 1910s. The early part of the period lasted 25 years and ended around 1935, when modern art was referred to as, what [Greenberg](/wiki/Clement_Greenberg "Clement Greenberg") called the [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde").

The 1913 [Armory Show](/wiki/Armory_Show "Armory Show") in New York City displayed the contemporary work of European artists, as well as Americans. The [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionist "Impressionist"), [Fauvist](/wiki/Fauvist "Fauvist") and [Cubist](/wiki/Cubist "Cubist") paintings startled many American viewers who were accustomed to more conventional art. However, inspired by what they saw, many American artists were influenced by the radical and new ideas.

The early 20th century was marked by the exploration of different techniques and ways of artistic expressiveness. Many American artists like [Wilhelmina Weber](/wiki/Wilhelmina_Weber_Furlong "Wilhelmina Weber Furlong"), [Man Ray](/wiki/Man_Ray "Man Ray"), [Patrick Henry Bruce](/wiki/Patrick_Henry_Bruce "Patrick Henry Bruce"), [Gerald Murphy](/wiki/Gerald_Murphy "Gerald Murphy") and others went to Europe, notably Paris, to make art. The formation of various artistic assemblies led to the multiplicity of meaning in the visual arts. The [Ashcan School](/wiki/Ashcan_School "Ashcan School") gathered around [realism](/wiki/Realism_(visual_arts) "Realism (visual arts)") ([Robert Henri](/wiki/Robert_Henri "Robert Henri") or [George Luks](/wiki/George_Luks "George Luks")); the [Stieglitz](/wiki/Alfred_Stieglitz "Alfred Stieglitz") circle glorified abstract visions of New York City ([Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber_(artist) "Max Weber (artist)"), [Abraham Walkowitz](/wiki/Abraham_Walkowitz "Abraham Walkowitz")); color painters evolved in direction of the colorful, abstract "[synchromies](/wiki/Synchromism "Synchromism")" ([Stanton Macdonald-Wright](/wiki/Stanton_Macdonald-Wright "Stanton Macdonald-Wright") and [Morgan Russell](/wiki/Morgan_Russell "Morgan Russell")), whereas [precisionism](/wiki/Precisionism "Precisionism") visualized the industrialized landscape of America in the form of sharp and dynamic geometrization ([Joseph Stella](/wiki/Joseph_Stella "Joseph Stella"), [Charles Sheeler](/wiki/Charles_Sheeler "Charles Sheeler"), [Morton Livingston Schamberg](/wiki/Morton_Livingston_Schamberg "Morton Livingston Schamberg"), [Charles Rosen](/wiki/Charles_Rosen_(painter) "Charles Rosen (painter)"), and [Charles Demuth](/wiki/Charles_Demuth "Charles Demuth")). Eventually artists like [Charles Burchfield](/wiki/Charles_Burchfield "Charles Burchfield"), [Marsden Hartley](/wiki/Marsden_Hartley "Marsden Hartley"), [Stuart Davis](/wiki/Stuart_Davis_(painter) "Stuart Davis (painter)"), [Arthur Dove](/wiki/Arthur_Dove "Arthur Dove"), [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe") who was thought of as the mother of American Modernism, [John Marin](/wiki/John_Marin "John Marin"), [Arthur Beecher Carles](/wiki/Arthur_Beecher_Carles "Arthur Beecher Carles"), [Alfred Henry Maurer](/wiki/Alfred_Henry_Maurer "Alfred Henry Maurer"), [Andrew Dasburg](/wiki/Andrew_Dasburg "Andrew Dasburg"), [James Daugherty](/wiki/James_Daugherty "James Daugherty"), [John Covert](/wiki/John_Covert_(painter) "John Covert (painter)"), [Henrietta Shore](/wiki/Henrietta_Shore "Henrietta Shore"), [William Zorach](/wiki/William_Zorach "William Zorach"), [Marguerite Thompson](/wiki/Marguerite_Zorach "Marguerite Zorach") (Zorach), [Manierre Dawson](/wiki/Manierre_Dawson "Manierre Dawson"), [Arnold Friedman](/wiki/Arnold_Friedman "Arnold Friedman") and [Oscar Bluemner](/wiki/Oscar_Bluemner "Oscar Bluemner") ushered in the era of [Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") to the [New York School](/wiki/New_York_School_(art) "New York School (art)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/THOMAS_HART_BENTON_-_NOON.jpg/250px-THOMAS_HART_BENTON_-_NOON.jpg)](/wiki/File:THOMAS_HART_BENTON_-_NOON.jpg)

*Noon* 1939 [American Regionalism](/wiki/Regionalism_(art) "Regionalism (art)") by [Thomas Hart Benton](/wiki/Thomas_Hart_Benton_(painter) "Thomas Hart Benton (painter)")

The shift of focus and multiplicity of subjects in the visual arts is also a hallmark of American modernist art. Thus, for example, the group [The Eight](/wiki/Ashcan_School "Ashcan School") brought the focus on the modern city, and placed emphasis on the diversity of different classes of citizens. But the late 1920s and the 1930s belonged to two movements in American painting, [Regionalism](/wiki/Regionalism_(art) "Regionalism (art)") and [Social Realism](/wiki/Social_Realism "Social Realism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] American Regionalism became prominent during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and the artists opposed "international urban modernism" as promoted in New York by [Alfred Stieglitz](/wiki/Alfred_Stieglitz "Alfred Stieglitz").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

American Modernism artists like [Charles Demuth](/wiki/Charles_Demuth "Charles Demuth"), who created his masterpiece *I Saw The Figure Five in Gold* in 1928, [Morton Schamberg](/wiki/Morton_Schamberg "Morton Schamberg") and [Charles Sheeler](/wiki/Charles_Sheeler "Charles Sheeler") were closely related to the [Precisionist movement](/wiki/Precisionism "Precisionism"). Sheeler typically painted cityscapes and industrial architecture as exemplified by his painting *Amoskeag Canal* 1948.

#### Modernist painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modernist painting")]

Main articles: [Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism"), [History of painting](/wiki/History_of_painting "History of painting"), and [Western painting](/wiki/Western_painting "Western painting")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Brooklyn_Museum_-_The_Old_Ships_Draw_to_Home_Again_-_Jonas_Lie_-_overall.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_The_Old_Ships_Draw_to_Home_Again_-_Jonas_Lie_-_overall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_The_Old_Ships_Draw_to_Home_Again_-_Jonas_Lie_-_overall.jpg)

*The Old Ships Draw to Home Again* 1920 [American Realism](/wiki/American_Realism "American Realism") by [Jonas Lie](/wiki/Jonas_Lie_(painter) "Jonas Lie (painter)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/df/Me_and_the_Moon.jpg/250px-Me_and_the_Moon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Me_and_the_Moon.jpg)

[Arthur Dove](/wiki/Arthur_Dove "Arthur Dove") *Me and the Moon* 1937

[Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe"), known as the "Mother of American modernism",[[6]](#cite_note-6) has been a major figure in American Modernism since the 1920s. She has received widespread recognition, for challenging the boundaries of modern American artistic style. She is chiefly known for paintings of flowers, rocks, shells, animal bones and landscapes in which she synthesized abstraction and representation.[[7]](#cite_note-7) She is among those American modernism artists, who visualized economic status through the subjects of performance and [leisure](/wiki/Leisure "Leisure"). While [Greta Grossman](/wiki/Greta_Grossman "Greta Grossman"), [Louisa Keyser](/wiki/Louisa_Keyser "Louisa Keyser"), [Maurice Martiné](/w/index.php?title=Maurice_Martin%C3%A9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maurice Martiné (page does not exist)"), [Isamu Noguchi](/wiki/Isamu_Noguchi "Isamu Noguchi"), [Horace Pippin](/wiki/Horace_Pippin "Horace Pippin"), and [Joseph Stella](/wiki/Joseph_Stella "Joseph Stella") painted rapid changes in American society through the subjects of [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), global war, [industry](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), and migration.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Arthur Dove](/wiki/Arthur_Dove "Arthur Dove") used a wide range of media, sometimes in unconventional combinations to produce his abstractions and his abstract landscapes. *Me and the Moon* from 1937 is a good example of an Arthur Dove abstract landscape and has been referred to as one of the culminating works of his career.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Dove did a series of experimental collage works in the 1920s. He also experimented with techniques, combining paints like hand mixed oil or tempera over a [wax emulsion](/wiki/Wax_emulsion "Wax emulsion").

African-American painter [Aaron Douglas](/wiki/Aaron_Douglas_(artist) "Aaron Douglas (artist)") (1899-1979) is one of the best-known and most influential African-American modernist painters. His works contributed strongly to the development of an aesthetic movement that is closely related to distinct features of African-American heritage and culture. Douglas influenced African-American visual arts especially during the [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance").

One of Douglas' most popular paintings is *The Crucifixion*. It was published in James Weldon Johnson's *God's Trombones* in 1927. The crucifixion scene that is depicted in the painting shows several elements that constitute Douglas' art: clear-cut delineation, change of shadows and light, stylized human bodies and geometric figures as concentric circles in contrast to linear forms. The painting's theme resembles not only the biblical scene but can also be seen as an allusion to African-American religious tradition: the oversized, dark Jesus is bearing his cross, his eyes directed to heaven from which light is cast down onto his followers. Stylized Roman soldiers are flanking the scene with their pointed spears. As a result, the observer is reminded for instance of the African-American gospel tradition but also of a history of suppression. [Beauford Delaney](/wiki/Beauford_Delaney "Beauford Delaney"), [Charles Alston](/wiki/Charles_Alston "Charles Alston"), [Jacob Lawrence](/wiki/Jacob_Lawrence "Jacob Lawrence") and [Romare Bearden](/wiki/Romare_Bearden "Romare Bearden") were also important [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") [Modernist](/wiki/Modernist "Modernist") painters that inspired generations of artists that followed them.

#### Modernist photography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modernist photography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Tina_Modotti_with_her_arms_raised_-_Edward_Weston_restoration.jpg/250px-Tina_Modotti_with_her_arms_raised_-_Edward_Weston_restoration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tina_Modotti_with_her_arms_raised_-_Edward_Weston_restoration.jpg)

*Tina Modotti with her arms raised* by [Edward Weston](/wiki/Edward_Weston "Edward Weston") circa 1921

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Pueblo_Acoma.jpg/250px-Pueblo_Acoma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pueblo_Acoma.jpg)

*Acoma Pueblo* by [Ansel Adams](/wiki/Ansel_Adams "Ansel Adams") 1941-1942

At the beginning of American modernism, photography still struggled to be recognized as a form of art. The photographer [Alfred Stieglitz](/wiki/Alfred_Stieglitz "Alfred Stieglitz") described it as: "Artists who saw my earlier photographs began to tell me that they envied me; that they felt my photographs were superior to their paintings, but that, unfortunately, photography was not an art. I could not understand why the artists should envy me for my work, yet, in the same breath, decry it because it was machine-made." (Stieglitz:8). In 1902, Stieglitz founded the [Photo-Secession](/wiki/Photo-Secession "Photo-Secession") group with members such as [Edward Steichen](/wiki/Edward_Steichen "Edward Steichen"), [Gertrude Käsebier](/wiki/Gertrude_K%C3%A4sebier "Gertrude Käsebier") and [Clarence Hudson White](/wiki/Clarence_Hudson_White "Clarence Hudson White"), which had the objective of raising the standard and increasing the awareness of art photography. At that point, their main style was *[pictorialist](/wiki/Pictorialist "Pictorialist")*, which was known for modifying photos through soft focus, special filters or exotic printing processes, to imitate the style of paintings and etchings of that time. For means of publication, Stieglitz, as the driving force of the movement, started the magazine *[Camera Work](/wiki/Camera_Work "Camera Work")*, in which he published artists he felt represented the movement. He also ran three galleries one after another, namely "291" (1905–1917), "The Intimate Gallery" (1925–1929) and "An American Place" (1929–1947). Especially 291 served as a meeting point for artists and writers and was the first to exhibit the early modernist art works of European artists, such as [Henri Matisse](/wiki/Henri_Matisse "Henri Matisse"), [Auguste Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin"), [Henri Rousseau](/wiki/Henri_Rousseau "Henri Rousseau"), [Paul Cézanne](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9zanne "Paul Cézanne"), and [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso"), in the United States. A further link to the European avant-garde was established by [Man Ray](/wiki/Man_Ray "Man Ray"). Born in America and inspired by the work he saw in Stieglitz' galleries, Ray emigrated to Paris in 1921 and together with artists of the European [Dada](/wiki/Dada "Dada") and [Surrealist](/wiki/Surrealist "Surrealist") movements created new photographic techniques such as [rayographs](/wiki/Rayographs "Rayographs") (placing objects directly on photosensitive paper).

In the early 1920s, photographers moved towards what they called *[straight photography](/wiki/Straight_photography "Straight photography")*. In contrast to the pictorialist style, they now rejected any kind of manipulation in the photographic process (e.g., soft lens, special developing or printing methods) and tried to use the advantages of the camera as a unique medium for capturing reality. Their motifs were supposed to look as objective as possible. Turning the focus away from classic portraiture and the pictorialist style, the photographers started using their pictures as means for representing the harsh realities of everyday life, but at the same time tried to search for the beauty in the detail or the overall aesthetical structure. Machines and factory work, sky scrapers and technical innovations became prominent motifs.
In 1932 some younger photographers (e.g. [Ansel Adams](/wiki/Ansel_Adams "Ansel Adams"), [Imogen Cunningham](/wiki/Imogen_Cunningham "Imogen Cunningham"), [Willard Van Dyke](/wiki/Willard_Van_Dyke "Willard Van Dyke"), [Edward Weston](/wiki/Edward_Weston "Edward Weston")) started [Group f/64](/wiki/Group_f/64 "Group f/64") based on the ideals of straight photography, which became the most progressive association of its time.

## American modernist literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: American modernist literature")]

American modernist literature was a dominant trend in American literature between [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The modernist era highlighted innovation in the form and language of poetry and prose, as well as addressing numerous contemporary topics, such as race relations, gender and the human condition. Many American modernists became expatriated in Europe during this time, often becoming stalwarts in the European movement, as was the case for [T. S. Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot"), [Ezra Pound](/wiki/Ezra_Pound "Ezra Pound") and [Gertrude Stein](/wiki/Gertrude_Stein "Gertrude Stein"). These writers were often known as the [Lost Generation](/wiki/Lost_Generation "Lost Generation").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

As a reaction to this trend, many American authors and poets began a trend of 'nativism', seeking to represent the modern American experience in America. Notable contributors to this trend include [William Carlos Williams](/wiki/William_Carlos_Williams "William Carlos Williams"), [Wallace Stevens](/wiki/Wallace_Stevens "Wallace Stevens") and [Marianne Moore](/wiki/Marianne_Moore "Marianne Moore"). These poets were often critical of the works of expatriate writers such as Eliot and Pound, as evidenced by poems like [Spring and All](/wiki/Spring_and_All "Spring and All").

Influenced by the first World War, many American modernist writers explored the psychological wounds and spiritual scars of the war experience. The economic crisis in America at the beginning of the 1930s also left a mark on literature, such as [John Steinbeck](/wiki/John_Steinbeck "John Steinbeck")'s *[The Grapes of Wrath](/wiki/The_Grapes_of_Wrath "The Grapes of Wrath")*. A related issue is the loss of self and need for self-definition, as workers faded into the background of city life, unnoticed cogs within a machine yearning for self-definition. American modernists echoed the mid-19th-century focus on the attempt to "build a self"—a theme illustrated by Fitzgerald's *[The Great Gatsby](/wiki/The_Great_Gatsby "The Great Gatsby")*. Madness and its manifestations seems to be another favorite modernist theme, as seen in [Eugene O'Neill](/wiki/Eugene_O%27Neill "Eugene O'Neill")'s *[The Emperor Jones](/wiki/The_Emperor_Jones "The Emperor Jones")*, Hemingway's *[The Battler](/wiki/The_Battler "The Battler")* and Faulkner's *[That Evening Sun](/wiki/That_Evening_Sun "That Evening Sun")*. Nevertheless, all these negative aspects led to new hopes and aspirations, and to the search for a new beginning, not only for the contemporary individuals, but also for the fictional characters in American modernist literature.

Modernist literature also allowed for the development of regional trends within American literature, including the [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance") and southern modernism. The Harlem Renaissance marked a rebirth for African American arts, centralized in the Harlem area of New York. Writers and thinkers such as [Alain Locke](/wiki/Alain_LeRoy_Locke "Alain LeRoy Locke"), [Claude McKay](/wiki/Claude_McKay "Claude McKay"), [Langston Hughes](/wiki/Langston_Hughes "Langston Hughes") and [Zora Neale Hurston](/wiki/Zora_Neale_Hurston "Zora Neale Hurston") were among the key figures of the movement. The movement was connected to a vogue for African American culture, as seen too in the popularity of Jazz music, with many writers financed by white patrons.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Many writers of this movement used modernist techniques to represent African American life,[[12]](#cite_note-12) for instance incorporating the rhythms of Jazz music and dialects of African American culture into poetry and prose.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Southern modernism similarly represented the life and unique experiences of the South using modernist aesthetics, with celebrated figures including [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner") and [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### The new criticism in America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The new criticism in America")]

From the 1930s to the 1960s, [New Criticism](/wiki/New_Criticism "New Criticism") became a critical force in the United States. It was the most powerful perspective in American literary criticism. The representatives were [John Crowe Ransom](/wiki/John_Crowe_Ransom "John Crowe Ransom"), [Allen Tate](/wiki/Allen_Tate "Allen Tate"), [Cleanth Brooks](/wiki/Cleanth_Brooks "Cleanth Brooks"), [Robert Penn Warren](/wiki/Robert_Penn_Warren "Robert Penn Warren"). "The influential critical methods these poet-professors developed emphasized the sharpening of close reading skills. New Criticism privileged the evaluation of poetry as the justification of literary scholarship". *Brooks and Warren's Understanding Poetry* (1938) became one of the most influential college poetry textbooks of the 1930s and was revised and reprinted well into the 1970s. (Morrisson: 29).

New criticism showed itself in such works as [Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot")'s and [Yeats](/wiki/Yeats "Yeats")' poems. "Poetry that best fit the aesthetic criteria of the New Critics was emphasized in important classroom teaching anthologies" (Morrisson: 29).
T. S. Eliot redefined tradition in his essay "Tradition and the Individual Talent". He formulated such critical concepts as "objective correlative", and rethought the literary canon in his elevation of Jacobean drama and metaphysical poetry. His work had a fundamental influence on New Criticism in America.

## Architecture and space

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_modernism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Architecture and space")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Lloyd_Wright_at_piano_at_Ocotillo.jpg/250px-Lloyd_Wright_at_piano_at_Ocotillo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lloyd_Wright_at_piano_at_Ocotillo.jpg)

The cabin of [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright") at Ocotillo, Chandler Arizona in 1929

The United States played a great role in the modernism movement concerning new advanced building and construction technologies.
Among construction innovations there are such materials as iron, steel and reinforced concrete.

[Louis Henry Sullivan](/wiki/Louis_Henry_Sullivan "Louis Henry Sullivan") headed the so-called Chicago school of architecture, which was distinct by its development of functional design along with modern materials. Sullivan's follower [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright") developed a new and original approach to residential design before World War I, which became known as the "prairie style." It combined [open planning](/wiki/Open_plan "Open plan") principles with horizontal emphasis, asymmetrical facade elevations, and broad, sheltering roofs. The Robie House in Chicago (1909) and the Guggenheim Museum in New York City (1946-1959) are two of his seminal works.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Wright became a renowned architect and his architectural designs were built in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), and the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area"). He disdained [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), but was taken by San Francisco which he regarded as *beautiful*.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In his works Wright moved closer and closer to an earth-bound sense of natural form, using rough-hewn stone and timber and aiming always in his houses to achieve an effect of intimate and protective shelter.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Jfader_barca_pavillion.jpg/250px-Jfader_barca_pavillion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jfader_barca_pavillion.jpg)

The *German Pavillion* in Barcelona, Spain 1929 by [Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Mies_van_der_Rohe "Mies van der Rohe")

Foreign-born architects as [Richard Neutra](/wiki/Richard_Neutra "Richard Neutra"), [Rudolf Schindler](/wiki/Rudolph_Schindler_(architect) "Rudolph Schindler (architect)"), and [William Lescaze](/wiki/William_Lescaze "William Lescaze") during the 1920s played a great role in development of American architecture performing later a style, which got the name of [international style](/wiki/International_style_(architecture) "International style (architecture)") and was reflected in the design of corporate office buildings after World War II. Such buildings as [Skidmore, Owings and Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_and_Merrill "Skidmore, Owings and Merrill")'s [Lever House](/wiki/Lever_House "Lever House") (1952) and [Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Mies_van_der_Rohe "Mies van der Rohe")'s [Seagram Building](/wiki/Seagram_Building "Seagram Building") (1956–58) in New York City are the examples of this new style. When such famous Europeans as [Walter Gropius](/wiki/Walter_Gropius "Walter Gropius") and Mies immigrated to the United States, many American architectural schools went under the influence of the traditions of the [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus") in Germany.
